title: Pistis Christou in Galatians 3:22
premise: In Galatians 3:22-26, the phrase πίστις Χριστοῦ in verse 22 admits several materially different semantic readings within Paul's immediate argument.
context_note: The map distinguishes an objective reading, a Christ-centered subjective or participatory reading, an attributive system reading, and an eschatological-event reading. Grasso's system and Schliesser's event remain distinct. An answer maps here only if it clearly makes Christ’s faith the semantic and explanatory center. A merely plenary, intentionally ambiguous, or evenly combined subjective-objective answer remains null. Coding follows the phrase's semantic referent, not the response's wider soteriology.
what_this_shows: If the precommitted Rule 3 threshold is met, this candidate can illustrate dispersion across several sourced semantic readings of one Pauline phrase in sampled answers.
what_this_does_not_show: It does not settle the exegesis, measure scholarly prevalence, establish that each model has one stable Pauline theology, or turn panel disagreement into evidence of truth.

selection.disclosure: The precommitted priority Rule 3 divergence candidate. It may be selected only if it meets the unchanged frozen divergence threshold after blinded review; the fallback is not run if this candidate qualifies.

prompt_variants.label: Default wording
prompt_variants.user_prompt: In Galatians 3:22-26, what does Paul most likely mean by πίστις Χριστοῦ in verse 22 when the phrase is read within its immediate argument? State one best-supported primary interpretation and explain the decisive grammatical, lexical, and contextual evidence.

position_map.label: Believers' faith in Christ
position_map.summary: The phrase denotes believers' faith or trust directed toward Christ.
position_map.attestation: Matlock's direct analysis of Galatians 3:22 argues that the Christ-faith phrase and the clause about those who believe both concern believers' faith, selecting the objective-genitive reading. The SBL Greek New Testament supplies the base text for the immediate argument.

sources.claim_supported: Matlock argues that Galatians 3:22 coordinates the promise through faith in Jesus Christ with its reception by those who believe, and explicitly concludes that both clauses concern believers' faith and select the objective-genitive reading.
sources.title: The Rhetoric of πίστις in Paul: Galatians 2.16, 3.22, Romans 3.22, and Philippians 3.9
sources.citation: R. Barry Matlock, “The Rhetoric of πίστις in Paul: Galatians 2.16, 3.22, Romans 3.22, and Philippians 3.9,” Journal for the Study of the New Testament 30, no. 2 (2007): 173-203, especially 187-193 and decisively 192, DOI 10.1177/0142064X07084775.

sources.claim_supported: The SBL Greek New Testament provides the base Greek text of Galatians 3:22-26, including πίστις Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ in verse 22 and the coming and revelation language of faith in verses 23-25.
sources.title: The Greek New Testament: SBL Edition, Galatians 3:22-26
sources.citation: Michael W. Holmes, ed., The Greek New Testament: SBL Edition (Atlanta: Society of Biblical Literature; Bellingham, WA: Lexham Press, 2010), Galatians 3:22-26, hosted by Bible Gateway.

position_map.label: Christ's own faith or faithfulness
position_map.summary: The phrase primarily denotes Christ’s own faith or faithfulness. This includes concentric or participatory accounts in which believers’ answering faith derives from and shares in Christ’s faith.
position_map.attestation: Hooker argues directly from Galatians 3:22 that the phrase refers to Christ's faith, then develops a concentric and participatory account in which Christ's faith remains primary while believers' answering faith derives from and shares in it.

sources.claim_supported: Hooker's Galatians 3 analysis concludes that verse 22 refers to Christ's own faith and that believers share in it; her conclusion defines the phrase concentrically, beginning from Christ's faith while necessarily including believers' answering faith.
sources.title: ΠΙΣΤΙΣ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ
sources.citation: Morna D. Hooker, “ΠΙΣΤΙΣ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ,” New Testament Studies 35, no. 3 (1989): 321-342, especially 329-331 and 340-342, DOI 10.1017/S0028688500016817.

position_map.label: Christ-defined system of faith
position_map.summary: The phrase denotes the Christ-defined or Christ-centered faith, understood as a system or body of belief, not a disposition exercised by either believer or Christ.
position_map.attestation: Grasso defines a third view as the Christ-centered content or system of faith, applies it directly to Galatians 3:22, and concludes that this attributive analysis is the most linguistically plausible reading.

sources.claim_supported: Grasso argues that πίστις χριστοῦ is best analyzed attributively as the Christ-centered content or system of faith and applies that reading directly to Galatians 3:22.
sources.title: A Linguistic Analysis of πίστις χριστοῦ: The Case for the Third View
sources.citation: Kevin Grasso, “A Linguistic Analysis of πίστις χριστοῦ: The Case for the Third View,” Journal for the Study of the New Testament 43, no. 1 (2020): 108-144, especially 113, 136-137, and 140, DOI 10.1177/0142064X20949385.

position_map.label: Christ-faith as eschatological event
position_map.summary: In the immediate argument, faith is a newly arrived and revealed reality, not an individual disposition or a belief system.
position_map.attestation: Schliesser reads the coming and revelation of faith in Galatians 3:23-26 as a trans-subjective eschatological event. He applies that account back to Christ-faith in verse 22 and distinguishes it from Christ's or believers' individual dispositions.

sources.claim_supported: Schliesser argues that Galatians 3:22-26 presents Christ-faith as a newly arrived, revealed, trans-subjective eschatological event rather than an individual disposition of Christ or a believer.
sources.title: ‘Christ-Faith’ as an Eschatological Event (Galatians 3.23-26): A ‘Third View’ on Πίστις Χριστοῦ
sources.citation: Benjamin Schliesser, “'Christ-Faith' as an Eschatological Event (Galatians 3.23-26): A 'Third View' on Πίστις Χριστοῦ,” Journal for the Study of the New Testament 38, no. 3 (2016): 277-300, especially 277-278, 290-294, and decisively 291 and 293-294, DOI 10.1177/0142064X15621654.
